title: Mixed OOP Problems
description: Full-program analysis and exam-style questions that synthesize every OOP concept — inheritance chains, polymorphism, static vs instance, exception handling, collections, and generics — exactly as they appear in CSE110 finals.

sections.content: 🎯 Why This Chapter Is Your Exam Battleground

CSE110 final exams at East West University do not test topics in isolation. A single question can involve a class hierarchy with `abstract` methods, a `static` counter, a `try/catch` block, and an `ArrayList` — all at once. This chapter is a simulation of that reality. Every exercise here is a **full-program trace** or **debug mission** that requires you to hold the entire object model in your head simultaneously. The skill being tested is not memorizing syntax — it is **reasoning about object state, dispatch order, and program flow under pressure**.

sections.label: Exam Strategy
sections.content: When tracing a multi-class program: (1) identify all **static** initializers and fields first — they run before any constructor. (2) Draw a quick **object diagram** on your answer sheet showing which reference points to which heap object. (3) Resolve every method call by asking: *what is the runtime type of this reference?* — that decides dynamic dispatch.
The table below shows which CSE110 concepts tend to appear **together** in the same exam question, and which chapters they draw from. Use it to identify your weak links before sitting the exam.

rows: extends, super(), constructor chaining, method hiding
abstract class, interface, default method, multiple types
private fields, getters/setters, static counter, this
all of the above woven into one 40–60 line program

sections.content: Before the exercises, review the two most commonly mis-traced features in full-program questions: **constructor chaining** (who calls whom, and in what order) and **static vs instance state** (shared across all objects vs owned per object).

rows: Must be first statement in child constructor; calls parent constructor
Forgetting parent constructor runs before child body
Calls another constructor in the same class; must be first statement
Combining super() and this() in the same constructor — illegal
One copy shared by all instances; survives object deletion
Thinking each object has its own copy
No `this`; cannot access instance fields directly
Calling instance method from static context without an object
Runs before every constructor body, after field defaults
Forgetting it runs even for constructors that call this()
Runs once when class is first loaded, before any constructor
Assuming it runs per object creation
Calling overridden method via parent ref
Resolved at compile to parent signature
Dispatched at runtime to child impl
Accessing hidden static method via parent ref

sections.label: The #1 Exam Mistake: Confusing Static Binding with Dynamic Dispatch
sections.content: If `Parent p = new Child()` and both classes define a **static** method `show()`, calling `p.show()` invokes **Parent's** version — static methods are bound at compile time, not dispatched at runtime. But if `show()` is an **instance** method with `@Override`, `p.show()` invokes **Child's** version. This distinction kills marks every semester.

Multi-class programs often pass exceptions **up the call stack**. The rule is simple: if a method does not catch an exception, it propagates to the caller. `finally` always executes — even if a `return` statement is inside the `try` or `catch` block. The only exceptions are `System.exit()` and JVM crashes.

sections.title: Exception Propagation & Finally Guarantee

sections.label: finally + return Trap
sections.content: If both `catch` and `finally` contain a `return` statement, the `finally` return **overrides** the `catch` return. The value from `catch` is silently discarded. This is a known exam trick — always check if `finally` has a `return`.

The following exercises are modeled on actual CSE110 exam formats. Each involves at least three OOP concepts working simultaneously. Work through them **without running the code first** — tracing by hand is the exam skill being built.

sections.title: Exercise 1: Inheritance Chain with Static Counter and Constructor Order
sections.answer:
Vehicle: Generic
Vehicle: Toyota
Car: 4 doors
Vehicle: Tesla
Car: 4 doors
ElectricCar: 500km
Car-Tesla-4-EV-500
3
sections.explanation:
**Step 1 — `new Vehicle("Generic")`:** Calls Vehicle constructor directly. Prints `Vehicle: Generic`. `count` becomes 1.

**Step 2 — `new Car("Toyota", 4)`:** Car constructor calls `super("Toyota")` first → Vehicle constructor runs, prints `Vehicle: Toyota`, `count` becomes 2. Control returns to Car constructor body → prints `Car: 4 doors`.

**Step 3 — `new ElectricCar("Tesla", 4, 500)`:** ElectricCar calls `super("Tesla", 4)` → Car calls `super("Tesla")` → Vehicle prints `Vehicle: Tesla`, `count` becomes 3 → back to Car body, prints `Car: 4 doors` → back to ElectricCar body, prints `ElectricCar: 500km`.

**Step 4 — `ref.info()`:** `ref` is of compile-time type `Vehicle` but runtime type `ElectricCar`. Dynamic dispatch selects `ElectricCar.info()`. That calls `super.info()` which calls `Car.info()` → `"Car-Tesla-4"` → appended with `"-EV-500"` → prints `Car-Tesla-4-EV-500`.

**Step 5 — `Vehicle.count`:** Static field, incremented 3 times. Prints `3`.

sections.title: Exercise 2: Abstract Class, Interface, and Default Method Collision
sections.answer:
Donald says Quack
Donald is a duck
Fly and Swim
Fly and Swim
true
true
sections.explanation:
**Line 1 — `a.info()`:** `a` is Animal reference, runtime type Duck. `info()` is not overridden in Duck so Animal's version runs. It calls `sound()` — dynamic dispatch hits Duck's `sound()` → `"Quack"`. Output: `Donald says Quack`.

**Line 2 — `f.describe()`:** `f` is Flyable reference, runtime Duck. `describe()` is an abstract interface method, Duck provides it → `Donald is a duck`.

**Line 3 — `f.move()`:** Both `Flyable` and `Swimmable` have a default `move()`. Duck **must** override `move()` to resolve the diamond conflict — and it does. Even through the `Flyable` reference, dynamic dispatch hits Duck's `move()` → `Fly and Swim`.

**Line 4 — `d.move()`:** Direct call, same result → `Fly and Swim`.

**Lines 5–6 — `instanceof`:** `a` has runtime type `Duck`. `Duck implements Swimmable` → `true`. `Duck implements Flyable` → `true`. The compile-time type of `a` (Animal) is irrelevant to `instanceof` — it checks the actual object.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Broken Generic Stack with Multiple Bugs
sections.error:
Bug 1 — Line 18: `data.get(data.size())` → runtime IndexOutOfBoundsException. Valid indices are 0 to size()-1; `data.size()` is one past the last element.
Bug 2 — Line 22: `static int size()` references instance field `data` from a static context → compile-time error: 'non-static variable data cannot be referenced from a static context'.
Bug 3 — Line 35: `s.pop()` called on empty stack after all 3 elements are removed → runtime RuntimeException: 'Stack is empty'.
sections.explanation:
**Bug 1 (runtime — IndexOutOfBoundsException):** ArrayList uses 0-based indexing. For a list of size N, valid indices are 0 through N-1. `data.get(data.size())` attempts index N which does not exist. Fix: `data.get(data.size() - 1)`.

**Bug 2 (compile-time — non-static field in static context):** The field `data` is an instance variable — it belongs to a specific Stack object. A `static` method has no `this` reference, so it cannot access `data`. Removing the `static` keyword makes `size()` an instance method with normal access to `this.data`.

**Bug 3 (runtime — RuntimeException on empty stack):** After three `pop()` calls, the stack is empty. The fourth `pop()` hits the `isEmpty()` guard and throws. Fix: either add a size check before the call or remove the extra pop. This models **defensive programming** — always verify state before calling methods that have preconditions.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Exception Propagation Across Methods with Finally
sections.answer:
Main start
L1 try
L2 try
L3 start
L2 finally
L1 catch: bad arg
L1 finally
Main end
sections.explanation:
**Main → level1():** Prints `Main start`, enters level1's try, prints `L1 try`, calls level2().

**level1 → level2():** Prints `L2 try`, calls level3().

**level2 → level3():** Prints `L3 start`, throws `IllegalArgumentException`.

**Back in level2:** Exception propagates. The `catch (NullPointerException)` does NOT match `IllegalArgumentException` — wrong type. The catch is skipped. But `finally` always runs → prints `L2 finally`. Exception continues propagating upward. `L2 after call` is never printed.

**Back in level1:** `catch (IllegalArgumentException e)` matches. Prints `L1 catch: bad arg`. Then `finally` runs → `L1 finally`. `L1 after call` is never printed.

**Back in main:** Exception was caught in level1, so main resumes normally. Prints `Main end`.

**Key insight:** `finally` runs even when the exception is NOT caught locally — it ran in level2 despite level2 not catching the exception. This is the exam-critical behavior.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Design a Bank Account Hierarchy with Encapsulation and Polymorphism
sections.approach:
1. Declare BankAccount as abstract with private fields — encapsulation means no direct field access from subclasses, only via inherited getters and a protected setter for balance.
2. Constructor validates balance; deposit validates amount — both throw IllegalArgumentException for bad input.
3. withdraw() is abstract — each subclass enforces its own rule.
4. SavingsAccount checks `getBalance() - amount >= 500` before withdrawing.
5. CheckingAccount checks `getBalance() - amount >= -200` before withdrawing.
6. In main(), use a BankAccount[] to call deposit() and withdraw() polymorphically — the runtime type determines which withdraw() runs.
7. Wrap one withdraw in try/catch to demonstrate graceful error handling.
sections.explanation:
**Encapsulation:** `balance` is private. Subclasses cannot assign `balance = x` directly. They must call the `protected setBalance()` method — this is intentional: it keeps mutation controlled while still allowing subclass withdraw implementations.

**Abstract + Polymorphism:** `accounts[0].withdraw(500.0)` dispatches to `SavingsAccount.withdraw()`. `accounts[1].withdraw(550.0)` dispatches to `CheckingAccount.withdraw()`. The caller code is identical — this is polymorphism.

**Exception design:** `IllegalArgumentException` (unchecked) for programming errors like negative deposits. `IllegalStateException` (unchecked) for valid operations that violate business rules. Both are subclasses of `RuntimeException` so they don't need `throws` declarations.

**toString():** `String.format("%.2f", balance)` formats to 2 decimal places. Overriding `toString()` from `Object` means `System.out.println(acc)` calls it automatically.

**Expected output:**
```
Caught: Cannot drop below minimum balance of 500.0
[Alice: 700.00]
[Bob: -150.00]
```

sections.title: Exercise 6: Broken Encapsulation and Inheritance Contract
sections.error:
Bug 1 — Line 8 (logical): `name = name` assigns the parameter to itself instead of to the field. The field `this.name` remains null. Fix: `this.name = name`.
Bug 2 — Line 23 (logical): Missing `@Override` annotation. While not a compile error, without it the compiler cannot catch accidental signature mismatches. It is a required best practice and EWU exam rubrics deduct marks for its absence on overriding methods.
Bug 3 — Line 27 (compile-time): `age` is `private` in Person. Subclasses cannot access private fields of the parent. Fix: either add a `protected` setter `setAge(int a)` in Person, or call a `birthday()` method defined in Person that increments age internally.
sections.explanation:
**Bug 1 (logical — shadow assignment):** Inside a constructor, a parameter named `name` shadows the field `name`. Without `this.`, the assignment `name = name` writes the parameter back to itself — the field stays `null`. This is one of the most common Java beginner bugs. Always use `this.field = parameter` in constructors.

**Bug 2 (logical — missing @Override):** Without `@Override`, if you accidentally misspell the method name or use a slightly wrong signature, the compiler treats it as a brand-new overloaded method rather than catching the mistake. EWU labs and exams require `@Override` on all intentional overrides.

**Bug 3 (compile-time — private access violation):** Private visibility is the strictest encapsulation level — not even subclasses can access it. The correct OOP solution is to expose a `protected setAge()` method in the parent class, keeping `age` private but allowing controlled modification through the class hierarchy.

sections.title: Exercise 7: Student Grade Book using Collections and Generics
sections.approach:
1. Student is a simple data class. Give it a meaningful toString().
2. GradeBook uses HashMap<T, ArrayList<Integer>> — each student maps to their list of scores. The bound `T extends Student` ensures T has at least Student's interface.
3. enroll() puts a new empty ArrayList if not already present.
4. addScore() validates 0-100, then appends.
5. getAverage() uses a loop to sum, divides by count. Guard against empty list.
6. getTopStudent() iterates all keys, tracks the key with max average.
7. In main(), demonstrate with 3 students and test getTopStudent().
sections.explanation:
**Generic bound `T extends Student`:** This means GradeBook can work with Student or any subclass (e.g., GraduateStudent). Inside the class, `T` is guaranteed to have all of Student's methods.

**`putIfAbsent`:** Atomic convenience method — only puts the new ArrayList if the key is not already present. Prevents accidentally resetting a student's scores on a second enroll call.

**`Map.Entry<T, ArrayList<Integer>>`:** The most reliable way to iterate both keys and values of a HashMap simultaneously. The alternative (`for T key : records.keySet()`) requires a second `records.get(key)` call.

**Autoboxing:** `scores.add(score)` autoboxes `int score` to `Integer`. The enhanced for loop `for (int s : scores)` unboxes back. This happens transparently but at a small cost — important to know for Ch18.

**Expected output:**
```
[2025-001] Ashik -> avg: 84.33
[2025-002] Raisa -> avg: 91.67
[2025-003] Kabir -> avg: 69.00
Top student: [2025-002] Raisa
```

rows: Which constructor calls super() or this()? What order do print statements execute?
Is the method static or instance? Static = hidden (parent version), instance = overridden (child version)
Does finally have a return? It overrides catch's return silently
`<T extends X>` means T has X's methods available inside the class
== compares references (same object). equals() compares content (String, Integer override it)
All objects share one static field. Incrementing it in constructor changes it for everyone
abstract method in parent called via parent reference → runtime type's impl runs

sections.label: Last-Minute Exam Checklist
sections.content: Before submitting any OOP trace: (1) Did you check whether each method is **static or instance**? (2) Did you follow **constructor chaining all the way up to Object**? (3) For every method call through a parent reference, did you ask **what is the actual runtime type**? (4) Did `finally` run even when you didn't catch the exception? (5) Are there any **String == comparisons** that should be `.equals()`?

items: **Constructor execution order:** static blocks → instance fields → instance initializer blocks → constructor body; in inheritance, parent constructor always completes before child body starts.
**Dynamic dispatch rule:** the method that runs is always determined by the **runtime type** of the object, not the compile-time type of the reference — applies only to instance methods, not static.

**Static method binding:** static methods are resolved at compile-time based on the **declared type** of the reference, not the runtime type — calling a static method through a parent reference always invokes the parent's version.
**Interface diamond default method:** if two interfaces provide the same default method, the implementing class **must override it** or the code will not compile.

**finally guarantee:** the finally block runs after every try/catch path — even if catch has a `return`, even if an exception propagates, even if no exception occurred. Only `System.exit()` bypasses it.
**private field access:** subclasses cannot directly access `private` fields of a parent; they must use `protected` or `public` getters/setters defined in the parent class.
**@Override importance:** always annotate overriding methods; the compiler then catches typos or signature mismatches that would otherwise silently create a new overloaded method.
**Generic bounded type `<T extends Base>`:** inside the generic class, T can call any method declared in Base; without a bound, only Object methods are available on T.
**HashMap.putIfAbsent(key, value):** adds the key-value pair only if the key is absent — cleaner and safer than a manual containsKey check for initialization.

**String equality:** use `.equals()` for content comparison; `==` only checks if two references point to the same object in memory — always produces surprising results with String literals vs `new String(...)`.

**Autoboxing pitfall:** `Integer a = 127; Integer b = 127; a == b` is `true` (cached); `Integer a = 200; Integer b = 200; a == b` is `false` (not cached) — always use `.equals()` for Integer comparison.

**Exception hierarchy for exams:** `Throwable → Error / Exception → RuntimeException → unchecked exceptions` — only checked exceptions (those that extend Exception but not RuntimeException) require `try/catch` or `throws` declaration.
